paragraphs.body: In the photo essay entitled “Streets of the Lost” written by Cheryl McCall and Photographed by Mary Ellen Mark, the audience observes a group of homeless teenagers throughout their days living in Seattle in 1983. The photo essay later gained so much attention that the creators decided to make a documentary on the same group of teens named “Streetwise.” Before viewing “Streetwise” in my intro to film class this past semester, my professor prefaced us with “This film makes me feel so many emotions, some of which I still can’t really explain.” I took note of what he said and when the film ended, it all made sense.

While reading the photo essay and watching the documentary, there was a sense of dread that sat in the back of my head. Maybe because they are so young and exposing themselves to arguably the worst parts of humanity. But maybe it is because their futures seemed predetermined. I could’ve googled every person introduced and been shown that so many of them had already passed away, some even shortly after the documentary was finished filming. Their situation and environment looked like something impossible to escape from. Constantly roaming streets asking for money, scavenging dumpsters for meals and getting into cars with strangers. Two of the teens McCall interviews in “Streets of the Lost” detail their living situation in the hotel and in “Streetwise” the audience gets to observe what they are describing “They sleep in a spooky, abandoned hotel that has no water or electricity, where they cleared one block-long hallway so they could roller-skate. Because the building is boarded up, they climb in at a second-story window” (McCall). There are no bed frames, just old mattresses on the wooden floors and no heating, it is an experience I could not begin to imagine.

annotations.text: But maybe it is because their futures seemed predetermined.
annotations.comment: This is an interesting idea. Let's talk about it :)

Also, you repeat the idea of predetermination at other points in the essay, which suggests that this idea is meaningful to you. Perhaps there's something about this notion at the heart of your essay? Let's explore :)
great quote, but don't forget quote burger introducing and explaining templates!

paragraphs.body: Something I can recognize, however, is the experience of getting older. Starting in September, it is light, and the kids talk of jumping trains and hanging out, the dangerous activities are in broad daylight and are concerning, but it is more enthralling to watch than depressing. There is still an innocence to it. As it continues though, we can observe the changing seasons. Leaves are starting to fall and the scenes become darker as the sun sets earlier. We also see some of the kids talking about leaving Seattle and discussing ageing out of the juvenile system. It starts to feel like there is a time bomb on their lives and they recognize it too. It is to stay on the street and go to jail or leave Watching a group of people enter a young adulthood of uncertainty and most likely tragedy was certainly the toughest part of viewing their lives. The director Martin Bell specifically does not comment on or include his voice in any of the interviews, so there is no agenda or opinion being pushed upon the audience. Despite their situations, the kids hardly ever complain, however, it is still easy to pick up on their anxiety about what is to come. One of the teens named DeWayne is shown discussing his future with his father who is incarcerated. He yells at him “You’re smart, you’re a good-looking kid, but you’re going to end up just like me.” He is urging his son to do the best that he can with his life because his time on the street is hurting his future. Unfortunately, later in the documentary, we see that DeWayne is not able to fulfill his dad’s wishes and will have to serve a sentence in juvie for selling marijuana. The news of his sentence is so difficult to process that the final scene in “Streetwise” is his casket after taking his own life.

annotations.text: The news of his sentence is so difficult to process that the final scene in “Streetwise” is his casket after taking his own life.
annotations.comment: gosh. heartbreaking. But a great detail.

paragraphs.body: When growing up, there is so much unknown and fear. Like I mentioned before, it is almost an understanding between the audience and the Teens that their futures are predetermined. They are striving for a simple life of being comfortable in a home, surrounded by people they care about. But because of their circumstances, this future is incredibly hard to obtain. It is as if the odds are against them. While reading “Streets of the Lost” and watching “Streetwise” I can recognize their fear and the specific times that they are realizing they need to either make a change or keep going with the practices of the street.

I am lucky enough that I do not have to feel like my future is predetermined in any way. Getting to experience both the photo essay and the documentary in a colligate classroom is what particularly made me feel this way. The divide between myself and the kids I observed through a screen is a huge one. Whether its through the way I was raised versus how they were. It is also marked by generations as both projects came out in the early 1980’s. But Martin Bell the director successfully makes it hard not to feel a sort of connection to them. Recognizing their laughing when they joke with their friends, or the way that they get awkward around each other. Both projects are extremely human and this is what makes them so enticing.

annotations.text: am lucky enough that I do not have to feel like my future is predetermined in any way. Getting to experience both the photo essay and the documentary in a colligate classroom is what particularly made me feel this way. The divide between myself and the kids I observed through a screen is a huge one.

paragraphs.body: “Streets of the Lost” and “Streetwise” are two prime examples of teenagers experiencing tragedy and major life changes for the first time. While my experience of leaving friends and family to go away to college is a different more privileged circumstance than theirs, there was so many little things that I personally could relate to and served as a reminder of what it is like to grow up and be forced into changing the way you live.

annotations.text: there was so many little things that I personally could relate to and served as a reminder of what it is like to grow up and be forced into changing the way you live.
